narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 42.50, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 2 and 1 respectively, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 41.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction firmly toward expecting you to pass, well above the typical score of around 37. Your undergraduate GPA was the next most influential strength, also tilting things in your favor compared to the average of 3.21, signaling solid prior academic performance. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—was the biggest drag, strongly contributing toward the failure prediction by highlighting a relative dip in performance that outweighed some of your other assets. While your middle income and full-time plans align with most applicants, they didn't shift the balance much here. Overall, your profile shows promise in test-taking and earlier academics but was held back by that later-year standing compared to peers who typically rank higher. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build on your already strong score, or focus on boosting your academic decile through targeted coursework or graduate prep programs that demonstrate upward momentum. Many students in similar situations reapply successfully after addressing weak spots—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and explore bridge programs or post-bac options tailored for Asian American applicants aiming for law. With your solid foundation, persistence could turn this into an opportunity for an even stronger application next time.
